claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 16 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 16 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 16 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions.

Let me start with: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child. I'd like to know if I'm eligible to claim Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To help determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions about your child and your circumstances.

**First, could you please tell me your child's name and age?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: My child's name is Alex, and they're 16 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Since Alex is 16 years old, there are specific rules that apply.

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** (For example, full-time non-advanced education such as school sixth form, A-levels, or approved training courses)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any education or training at the moment. They left school and aren't doing anything like that right now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Let me ask a follow-up question about Alex's current situation.

**Has Alex registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services?** (If so, and this happened within the last 20 weeks since leaving education, you may still be eligible for Child Benefit during that period)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they haven't registered with anything like that. They just left school and haven't signed up with the careers service or anything.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. I have a few more questions to complete the assessment.

**Is Alex currently working in paid employment for 24 hours or more per week?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't working at all right now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Let me ask one more important question:

**Is Alex currently receiving any benefits in their own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't receive any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Now I need to ask about your circumstances:

**Do you currently live in the UK, and does Alex live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - in Manchester actually. And yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you. Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment:

**Are you responsible for Alex - meaning you provide for their needs such as food, clothes, accommodation, and other living expenses?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, absolutely. I provide everything for Alex - their food, clothes, and all their living expenses.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Just one final question:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, I'm afraid **you are not currently eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why: While you would normally qualify for Child Benefit for a child under 16, different rules apply once a child turns 16. At 16 or over, you can still get Child Benefit if your child stays in approved education or training (and they're under 20). 

Since Alex has left school and is not currently in approved education or training, you would not qualify under this rule. There is one exception - you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks if Alex registers with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services, but Alex has not done this yet.

**However, Alex could take action to help you become eligible:** If Alex registers with a government-sponsored careers service (such as the National Careers Service), you may be able to claim Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks. Alternatively, if Alex returns to approved education or training, you would become eligible again.

I hope this helps clarify your situation. Please let me know if you have any questions.

reasonings: Alex is 16 years old and has left school. According to the Child Benefit rules, you normally still qualify for Child Benefit if your child is under 20 and they stay in approved education or training. However, Alex is not currently in approved education or training. While you can apply for Child Benefit to continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services, Alex has not registered with either of these. Additionally, the rules state you'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training, or starts getting certain benefits - but these are conditions for losing benefit, not gaining it. Since Alex is 16, not in approved education or training, and has not registered with a careers service or armed services, you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex at this time.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with their 16-year-old child Alex, provides for all of Alex's needs, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. However, because Alex has left education and is not in approved education or training, and has not registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services, the claimant does not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for a child aged 16 or over.

Since Alex is 16, not in approved education or training, and has not registered with a careers service or armed services, you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for Alex at this time.

However, because Alex has left education and is not in approved education or training, and has not registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services, the claimant does not meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for a child aged 16 or over.
